ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03411433 covers king crab as a food item; Alaskan King Crab (Paralithodes camtschaticus) is the species-level match within this parent class.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226803007 covers crab as a substance; no species-specific Alaskan King Crab code exists in SNOMED CT.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Alaskan King Crab at a Singapore seafood restaurant is approximately 100 g of cooked crab meat, often served as part of a shared seafood platter.

nutrient_source.title: Crustaceans, king crab, Alaska, cooked, moist heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15149)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for Alaska King Crab, cooked by moist heat, per 100 g edible portion. This is the canonical approved-source match for this food.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Alaskan King Crab in the University of Sydney GI Database. Shellfish and crustaceans contain negligible carbohydrate (0 g per 100 g); GI is estimated at 5 by analogy with other cooked crustaceans and protein-dominant seafood, consistent with the principle that foods with zero digestible carbohydrate have a GI approaching zero. Glycemic load per 100 g serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g = 0. gi_category is 'low' (GI ≤ 55).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (836 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Alaskan King Crab intake or choose preparations without added salt, as 100 g provides approximately 836 mg sodium — about 36% of the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero digestible carbohydrate and high lean protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Alaskan King Crab is an excellent protein source with zero carbohydrate, making it glycemically neutral and suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, sodium-rich preparations should be moderated to protect renal and cardiovascular health.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium and moderate cholesterol, offset by low saturated fat and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Alaskan King Crab provides lean protein and omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (0.54 g per 100 g) which are cardioprotective, but its high sodium content (836 mg per 100 g) warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease who must restrict sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Alaskan King Crab (Paralithodes camtschaticus) is a large cold-water crustacean harvested from the North Pacific Ocean and the Bering Sea off the coast of Alaska. It is prized for its sweet, delicate, and slightly briny white meat, which is firm yet tender in texture. In Singapore's Chinese seafood restaurants, Alaskan King Crab is typically served steamed with garlic and egg white, stir-fried with salted egg yolk, or prepared in a superior stock broth. The crab is sold live or pre-cooked and frozen, with the legs being the most commercially valued portion. Nutritionally, per 100 g of cooked meat, it provides approximately 97 kcal, 19.4 g of high-quality protein, and only 1.5 g of fat, making it one of the leanest luxury seafood options available. It contains zero carbohydrate and zero sugar, rendering it glycemically neutral and suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. It is an excellent source of vitamin B12 (9.78 mcg per 100 g, exceeding the adult RDA), zinc (7.62 mg), selenium (36.4 mcg), and phosphorus (280 mg). The primary clinical concern is its high sodium content (836 mg per 100 g), which is relevant for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Alaskan King Crab is a shellfish allergen and is not halal-certified in most Singapore contexts unless specifically sourced and certified.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy NDB 15149, which is an exact match for Alaska King Crab cooked by moist heat per 100 g edible portion. This is an approved primary source. GI is estimated by analogy due to zero carbohydrate content, which is a well-established and defensible approach for protein-dominant seafood.
notes: carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy — Alaskan King Crab contains no digestible carbohydrate; confirmed zero. sugar_g is confirmed zero for the same reason; confirmed zero. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero as crustaceans contain no dietary fibre; confirmed zero. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as reported by USDA SR Legacy; confirmed zero. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for this food item. Sodium content (836 mg per 100 g) reflects cooked moist-heat preparation and may vary with restaurant preparation methods. GI is estimated at 5 (effectively zero) as this food contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention. Halal status is not confirmed as king crab is a crustacean, which is considered haram in mainstream Islamic jurisprudence; is_halal is set to false. Restaurant context: served at Riverfront Seafood, Boat Quay, Singapore (field survey data source record). Price SGD 28.60 per 100 g portion as listed on field survey data.
